questions.q: Which condition makes $g = (g_v)_v$ an element of $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{A}_\mathbb{Q})$ rather than the naïve product?
choices: $g_p \in \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Z}_p)$ for all but finitely many primes $p$.
$g_v$ is upper-triangular at every finite place.
questions.hint: The restricted-product constraint is membership in the compact open $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Z}_p)$ at almost all $p$.
questions.explain: The restricted-product constraint is membership in the compact open $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Z}_p)$ at almost all $p$. Without it the topology would not be locally compact.

questions.q: Which of the following are open subgroups of $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{A}_\mathbb{Q})$?
questions.hint: $\mathrm{GL}_2(\hat{\mathbb{Z}})$ is compact open; multiplying by the open $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{R})^+$ stays open.
questions.explain: $\mathrm{GL}_2(\hat{\mathbb{Z}})$ is compact open; multiplying by the open $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{R})^+$ stays open. $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Q})$ is discrete (not open), and a classical congruence subgroup is a subgroup of $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$, not of the adelic group.

questions.q: Which factorisation of $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{A}_\mathbb{Q})$ separates archimedean and finite-adelic data?
questions.hint: The adèle ring splits as $\mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{A}_f$, and applying $\mathrm{GL}_2$ pointwise gives $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{R}) \times \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{A}_f)$.
questions.explain: The adèle ring splits as $\mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{A}_f$, and applying $\mathrm{GL}_2$ pointwise gives $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{R}) \times \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{A}_f)$.

questions.q: Strong approximation for $\mathrm{GL}_2$ at level $N$ asserts $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{A}_\mathbb{Q}) = \,?$
questions.hint: The full adelic group is the product of the (discrete) global rationals with the (open) connected archimedean component times an open compact $K_f$.
questions.explain: The full adelic group is the product of the (discrete) global rationals with the (open) connected archimedean component times an open compact $K_f$. This is the form that translates double cosets into $Y_0(N)$.

questions.q: What is the resulting double-coset description of $Y_0(N)$?
questions.hint: $\mathbb{H} \cong \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{R})^+ / Z(\mathbb{R})^+ \mathrm{SO}_2(\mathbb{R})$ and quotienting on both sides by $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Q})$ on the left and by $K_0(N)$ on the right…
questions.explain: $\mathbb{H} \cong \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{R})^+ / Z(\mathbb{R})^+ \mathrm{SO}_2(\mathbb{R})$ and quotienting on both sides by $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Q})$ on the left and by $K_0(N)$ on the right recovers $\Gamma_0(N) \backslash \mathbb{H}$.

questions.q: Why does strong approximation fail for the adelic group at $\mathrm{GL}_1$ (i.e. for $\mathbb{A}^\times$)?
choices: $\mathrm{GL}_1$ has non-trivial idèle class group $\mathbb{A}^\times/\mathbb{Q}^\times$ — connected components of finite-adelic data don't all collapse.
questions.hint: Strong approximation for $\mathrm{GL}_2$ uses that $\mathrm{SL}_2$ has trivial idèle class group; $\mathrm{GL}_1$'s class group is precisely $C_\mathbb{Q}$, which is non-trivial — so…
questions.explain: Strong approximation for $\mathrm{GL}_2$ uses that $\mathrm{SL}_2$ has trivial idèle class group; $\mathrm{GL}_1$'s class group is precisely $C_\mathbb{Q}$, which is non-trivial — so $\mathbb{A}^\times \ne \mathbb{Q}^\times \cdot \mathbb{R}^\times \cdot \hat{\mathbb{Z}}^\times$ in a single double coset.

questions.q: Which conditions are required of an automorphic form $\phi : \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Q}) \backslash \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{A}) \to \mathbb{C}$?
choices: Right $K$-finiteness under a maximal compact subgroup.
questions.hint: Compact support is too strong — it would rule out Eisenstein series.
questions.explain: Compact support is too strong — it would rule out Eisenstein series. The three required conditions are right $K$-finiteness, $\mathfrak{z}$-finiteness, and moderate growth.

questions.q: An automorphic form $\phi$ is a cusp form when:
choices: $\int_{\mathbb{Q} \backslash \mathbb{A}} \phi(n_x g)\,dx = 0$ for all $g$, where $n_x$ runs over the upper-triangular unipotent.
questions.hint: The Whittaker / constant-term integral over the unipotent radical of the standard Borel must vanish; this is the adelic translation of "$f$ vanishes at every cusp".
questions.explain: The Whittaker / constant-term integral over the unipotent radical of the standard Borel must vanish; this is the adelic translation of "$f$ vanishes at every cusp".

questions.q: Why is left-$\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Q})$-invariance built into the definition?
choices: Because the diagonal $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Q})$ is dense in $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{A})$.
Because $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Q})$ is discrete and we want functions on the quotient.
questions.hint: $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Q})$ embeds discretely in the locally compact $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{A})$, so the quotient $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Q}) \backslash \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{A})$ is the analogue of…
questions.explain: $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Q})$ embeds discretely in the locally compact $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{A})$, so the quotient $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Q}) \backslash \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{A})$ is the analogue of $\Gamma \backslash G$ in the classical setting.

questions.q: Given $f \in M_k(\Gamma_0(N))$ and the decomposition $g = \gamma g_\infty k_f$ from strong approximation, the adelic lift $\phi_f$ is defined by:
questions.hint: The slash-operator $f|_k g_\infty$ accounts for the weight-$k$ automorphy factor; evaluating at $i \in \mathbb{H}$ projects to $\mathbb{C}$.
questions.explain: The slash-operator $f|_k g_\infty$ accounts for the weight-$k$ automorphy factor; evaluating at $i \in \mathbb{H}$ projects to $\mathbb{C}$. The decomposition is well-defined modulo the stabiliser of $i$ inside $K_\infty K_f$.

questions.q: Under this dictionary, the right $K_0(N)$-invariant adelic forms of weight $k$ correspond to:
questions.hint: Right $K_0(N)$-invariance is exactly the level structure; combined with weight $k$ and the adelic transformation law, this carves out $M_k(\Gamma_0(N))$ on the classical side.
questions.explain: Right $K_0(N)$-invariance is exactly the level structure; combined with weight $k$ and the adelic transformation law, this carves out $M_k(\Gamma_0(N))$ on the classical side.

questions.q: What corresponds adelically to a classical cusp form $f \in S_k(\Gamma_0(N))$?
choices: An automorphic form whose constant term along the upper-unipotent vanishes.
questions.hint: Classical cuspidality (vanishing at all cusps) translates into the adelic constant-term condition $\int_{\mathbb{Q} \backslash \mathbb{A}} \phi(n_x g)\,dx = 0$.
questions.explain: Classical cuspidality (vanishing at all cusps) translates into the adelic constant-term condition $\int_{\mathbb{Q} \backslash \mathbb{A}} \phi(n_x g)\,dx = 0$.

questions.q: The spherical Hecke algebra $\mathcal{H}_p = C_c(\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Z}_p) \backslash \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Q}_p) / \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Z}_p))$ is, as an algebra:
choices: Commutative, generated by the characteristic functions of $\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Z}_p) \operatorname{diag}(p^a, p^b) \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Z}_p)$.
questions.hint: The Cartan / Smith decomposition shows the double cosets are indexed by $a \ge b$ in $\mathbb{Z}$; convolution makes the algebra commutative — Gelfand pair argument.
questions.explain: The Cartan / Smith decomposition shows the double cosets are indexed by $a \ge b$ in $\mathbb{Z}$; convolution makes the algebra commutative — Gelfand pair argument.

questions.q: The Satake isomorphism identifies $\mathcal{H}_p$ with:
choices: $\mathbb{C}[X^{\pm 1}, Y^{\pm 1}]^{S_2}$, symmetric Laurent polynomials in two variables.
questions.hint: The Satake transform sends Hecke functions to symmetric polynomials in the Satake parameters; the Weyl group of $\mathrm{GL}_2$ is $S_2$, hence the symmetrisation.
questions.explain: The Satake transform sends Hecke functions to symmetric polynomials in the Satake parameters; the Weyl group of $\mathrm{GL}_2$ is $S_2$, hence the symmetrisation.

questions.q: If $\pi_p$ is unramified with Satake parameters $(\alpha, \beta)$, the Hecke eigenvalue of $T_p$ on the new-vector is:
questions.hint: The classical Hecke eigenvalue $a_p$ relates to the Satake parameters by $a_p = (\alpha + \beta) p^{(k-1)/2}$ (or $\alpha + \beta$ in the unitary normalisation).
questions.explain: The classical Hecke eigenvalue $a_p$ relates to the Satake parameters by $a_p = (\alpha + \beta) p^{(k-1)/2}$ (or $\alpha + \beta$ in the unitary normalisation). Either form of the answer is acceptable; this option gives the classical weight-$k$ normalisation.

questions.q: A Whittaker functional on $\pi_v$ is a linear $W$-functional satisfying $W(\pi_v(n_x) v) = \,?$
choices: $\psi(x) W(v)$ for a fixed non-trivial additive character $\psi$
questions.hint: The defining $\psi$-equivariance is what distinguishes a Whittaker functional and forces uniqueness (up to scalar) for generic representations — local multiplicity-one.
questions.explain: The defining $\psi$-equivariance is what distinguishes a Whittaker functional and forces uniqueness (up to scalar) for generic representations — local multiplicity-one.

questions.q: Casselman's new-vector theorem says that for an irreducible admissible $\pi_p$, there is a unique vector (up to scalar) fixed by:
choices: $K_0(p^{c_p})$ for the smallest $c_p \ge 0$ admitting any fixed vector.
questions.hint: $c_p$ is the local conductor exponent.
questions.explain: $c_p$ is the local conductor exponent. Smaller $c_p$ would give a fixed vector of even lower level, contradicting minimality; larger gives a multidimensional space. Multiplying these gives the global conductor $N = \prod_p p^{c_p}$, which equals the classical level.

questions.q: Which classical theorem is the Whittaker / new-form story the adelic upgrade of?
choices: Dirichlet's theorem on primes in arithmetic progressions.
questions.hint: Atkin–Lehner singles out a unique normalised new-form at each level $N \mid M$ in the classical decomposition $S_k(\Gamma_0(M)) = \bigoplus_{N \mid M} S_k^{\mathrm{new}}(\Gamma_0(N))^{\dim}$ — the…
questions.explain: Atkin–Lehner singles out a unique normalised new-form at each level $N \mid M$ in the classical decomposition $S_k(\Gamma_0(M)) = \bigoplus_{N \mid M} S_k^{\mathrm{new}}(\Gamma_0(N))^{\dim}$ — the same statement, prime by prime, in the local Whittaker / Kirillov language.

questions.q: For an unramified $\pi_p$ with Satake parameters $(\alpha, \beta)$, the local $L$-factor is:
questions.hint: Each Satake parameter contributes a geometric series.
questions.explain: Each Satake parameter contributes a geometric series. The product of two such factors is the rank-2 Euler factor at $p$.

questions.q: When $\pi$ corresponds to a classical eigenform $f$ of weight $k$ and level $N$, the global automorphic $L$-function $L(s, \pi) = \prod_v L_v(s, \pi_v)$ equals:
choices: The Dirichlet $L$-function $L(s, \chi)$ of a character mod $N$.
The classical $L$-function $L(s, f)$ (up to a shift / archimedean factor).
questions.hint: The unramified local factors reproduce the Hecke Euler factors of $f$; the archimedean factor is a shifted gamma.
questions.explain: The unramified local factors reproduce the Hecke Euler factors of $f$; the archimedean factor is a shifted gamma. This is the dictionary that makes the adelic theory a true generalisation of the classical.

questions.q: Why is the adelic formulation necessary for higher rank $\mathrm{GL}_n$ rather than just $\mathrm{GL}_2$?
choices: Because $\mathbb{H}$ is only a symmetric space for $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{R})$.
Because higher-rank groups have more places of bad reduction than $\mathrm{GL}_2$.
Because higher-rank symmetric spaces support no classical functions.
Because Dirichlet series have no analogue beyond $\mathrm{GL}_1$.
questions.hint: $\mathbb{H} = \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{R})^+ / Z \mathrm{SO}_2$ is a special low-dimensional symmetric space; for $\mathrm{GL}_n$ with $n \ge 3$ the analogous symmetric space is non-Kähler and the…
questions.explain: $\mathbb{H} = \mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{R})^+ / Z \mathrm{SO}_2$ is a special low-dimensional symmetric space; for $\mathrm{GL}_n$ with $n \ge 3$ the analogous symmetric space is non-Kähler and the classical theory of holomorphic modular forms breaks down. Working directly on $\mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{Q}) \backslash \mathrm{GL}_n(\mathbb{A})$ avoids the issue.
